posts.content:
Someone just asked you to "just add a column" to the spreadsheet.

We need to talk about this.

Which column did they want you to "just add"?

A) One that requires rebuilding the entire data structure from scratch
B) One that needs a formula none of us fully understand yet
C) One that already exists on Sheet4 which nobody has opened in two years
D) The one that makes you realize the entire model needs to be rebuilt

Drop the "just add a column" story. How long did it actually take 👇
posts.first_comment:
Bonus — Mike Girvin (3,900+ YouTube tutorials) mapped out the exact framework for knowing when a formula fix is enough versus when the whole structure needs rethinking. Excel Flow Edition #28.

posts.content:
Hot take. Come at me.

The most valuable Excel skill is not XLOOKUP. Not Power Query. Not dynamic arrays.

It is the ability to build something someone else can use, understand, and fix without you in the room.

A) Agree — I have seen brilliant models become useless because only one person understood them
B) Disagree — technical depth is more valuable and documentation can come later
C) Both — you need the technical skills and the communication skills and neither alone is enough
D) I have been the only person who could fix something I built and I realize now that was a problem

Drop what you would add to a model right now to make it genuinely hand-off-ready 👇

Monday check-in. No judgment.

Which of these best describes your Excel right now?

A) Clean. Documented. A work of art I am proud to have open on my screen.
B) Functional. I know where everything is even if nobody else does.
C) Getting the job done. Please do not ask follow-up questions.
D) I made three decisions under deadline pressure today and I will deal with the consequences later.

Drop the one thing you are fixing before you close the laptop tonight 👇

What is the Excel feature you have been meaning to learn for over a year but keep pushing back because something more urgent always comes up?

A) Power Query — I know it would change everything but I keep saying next week
B) LAMBDA and custom functions — genuinely intimidating every time I look at it
C) Power Pivot and DAX — I know I need it for large datasets and I have not started
D) Dynamic arrays — I know FILTER and SEQUENCE exist and I am still doing it the old way

Vote and drop what specifically keeps getting in the way. Not the generic "not enough time." The actual thing.

Your conditional formatting is highlighting the wrong rows and you have been staring at it for 20 minutes.

The most common cause: your formula references are locking in the wrong direction.

When you write a conditional formatting rule that applies to an entire range Excel adjusts the row reference for each row. If you lock the row with a dollar sign the formula checks the same row for every cell. If you do not lock the column Excel shifts it sideways too.

The rule that highlights entire rows correctly:
=AND($C2="Overdue", $D2<>"Complete")

The dollar sign before C and D locks the column. No dollar sign before 2 lets the row shift for each row in your data. This is the single most common conditional formatting mistake and it produces exactly the symptom of "it only works on some rows."

Test your rule by clicking into Manage Rules and checking which cell the rule was written for. If it says $A$1 when your data starts at A2 the entire rule is offset by one row and nothing will highlight correctly.

Drop your conditional formatting problem below. Tell me what it is supposed to do and what it is actually doing and I will rewrite the rule in the comments.

→ Full conditional formatting guide in Excel Flow Edition #24
[link]

Real scenario from this week:

"I have a sales table and I need to show each rep's total, their percentage of overall sales, and their rank. I have been doing all three manually in separate columns."

Three formulas, none of which require manual updating:

Total by rep:
=SUMIF($A$2:$A$1000, E2, $B$2:$B$1000)
Where A is the rep column, B is the revenue column, and E2 is the rep name.

Percentage of total:
=F2/SUM($F$2:$F$20)
Format as percentage. The dollar signs lock the denominator so it always divides by the full total.

Rank:
=RANK(F2, $F$2:$F$20, 0)
0 means highest value equals rank 1. Use RANK.AVG for tied values.

Combine all three into one readable output:
="Rank "&RANK(F2,$F$2:$F$20,0)&" — "&TEXT(F2/SUM($F$2:$F$20),"0.0%")&" of total"

That produces "Rank 3 — 14.2% of total" directly in the cell. No helper columns. No manual updates. Add a row to your table and every number recalculates.

Drop the sales reporting formula you are currently building manually. Tell me what the output needs to look like and I will write it.

A) Built correctly from the start and I stand behind every single decision in it
B) Started correctly and then deadline happened
C) Inherited from someone who no longer works here
D) I know it works but I cannot fully explain why and that is between me and Excel

Drop the oldest formula in your current workbook that you have never touched because you are genuinely afraid of what happens if you do 👇

If your most important workbook got opened by a stranger right now with zero context — how long would it take them to understand what it does?

You need to extract specific text from a cell and every formula you have tried breaks on at least one row.

Extract text before a delimiter:
=LEFT(A2, FIND("-", A2)-1)
Returns everything before the first hyphen. Replace the hyphen with whatever your delimiter is.

Extract text after a delimiter:
=MID(A2, FIND("-", A2)+1, LEN(A2))
Returns everything after the first hyphen regardless of length.

Extract text between two delimiters:
=MID(A2, FIND("(",A2)+1, FIND(")",A2)-FIND("(",A2)-1)
Returns everything between parentheses. Works for brackets, quotes, or any pair of characters.

For messy data where the delimiter is inconsistent:
Use Flash Fill (Ctrl+E). Type what you want the first row to look like. Press Ctrl+E. Excel infers the pattern and fills the rest of the column without a formula.

Drop the text you are trying to extract below. Tell me what the cell contains and what you need to pull out and I will write the exact formula.

→ Text manipulation formulas in Excel Flow Edition #22
[link]

Real scenario:

"I have a workbook that gets emailed to 15 people every Monday. Every single one of them edits their own version and emails it back. By Tuesday I have 15 different files I have to manually combine into one."

This is a version control problem. Power Query solves it permanently.

Option 1 — SharePoint or OneDrive with a single shared file
Everyone edits the same file at the same time. Excel for Microsoft 365 handles concurrent editing natively. No combining required. This is the right answer if your company uses Microsoft 365.

Option 2 — Individual input files combined by Power Query
Each person fills in their own file in a consistent format. Store all 15 in one folder. Data → Get Data → From File → From Folder → Combine and Transform → build your cleaning steps once → Close and Load. Every Monday you click Refresh. The combined table updates in seconds.

Option 3 — A shared form with structured inputs
Use Microsoft Forms or a structured Excel table with locked formatting so people can only fill in specific cells. Eliminates formatting variation entirely.

Option 1 is the real fix if Microsoft 365 is available to you. Option 2 is the right workaround if it is not.

Drop your version consolidation situation. Tell me how many people are involved and what tools your company uses and I will tell you the fastest path.

B) My resume is slightly generous — I know enough to close the gaps when needed
C) My resume says Advanced — Excel has a different opinion about that
D) I have never been tested on anything I listed and I am not going to think about this too hard

Drop what you would actually need to learn before letting someone formally verify your Excel skills 👇

posts.content:
Someone on your team just showed you a 47-slide presentation built entirely from manually updated pivot tables. Different filter applied by hand on each slide. Refreshed manually every month.

What do you do?

A) Say nothing. It works. I pick my battles carefully.
B) Show them slicers. One click. Problem solved in eight minutes.
C) Build them a proper dashboard connected to one master pivot and save them two hours a month.
D) Quietly rebuild it correctly and present it as a small improvement.

Drop the manual Excel process you witnessed that you physically could not stay quiet about 👇

Hot take I want real pushback on.

Most Excel training programs teach people to be faster at doing the wrong things.

If you teach someone to write VLOOKUP faster you have helped them. If you never tell them that their unstructured data is the actual problem you have made them confidently wrong at higher speed.

A) Agree — technique without structure is just faster failure
B) Disagree — you teach the tool first and structural thinking follows with experience
C) Both — the best training covers formula AND file architecture from the very first session
D) It depends on the learner — some people need to do it wrong once before they understand why structure matters

Drop the Excel habit someone taught you that you later had to completely unlearn 👇

What is the Excel thing you learned by watching someone else that you had never seen in any tutorial?

A) A keyboard shortcut that made an entire category of work permanently faster
B) A formula approach that was completely different from how I had been doing it
C) A file organization habit I immediately copied and have used ever since
D) A way of thinking about a problem that changed how I structure models

Vote and drop the specific thing below. These threads are always the best ones of the week.

Your FILTER formula keeps throwing a #CALC! error or a #SPILL! error and you cannot figure out why.

#CALC! error means the filter returned zero results. FILTER does not know what to return when nothing matches.

Fix: add a third argument — the value to show when nothing matches.
=FILTER(A2:C100, B2:B100="West", "No results")

#SPILL! error means something is already sitting in a cell where the spilled results need to go.

Fix: check every cell below and to the right of your formula. Delete anything in the spill range. Even a single invisible space character will block the spill.

To find exactly what is blocking it: click the formula cell → click the blue dotted border that appears → Excel shows you the exact cell causing the blockage.

Drop your dynamic array error below. Tell me the formula and the error you are getting and I will diagnose it.

→ Dynamic arrays complete guide in Excel Flow Edition #27
https://substack.com/@rileystream/p-197635354

Real scenario:

"I need a report that compares this month's revenue to last month and to the same month last year. Right now I have three separate SUMIFS and I update the month reference manually every time."

Three formulas that update themselves automatically on the first of every month:

This month:
=SUMIFS(Revenue, Date, ">="&DATE(YEAR(TODAY()),MONTH(TODAY()),1), Date, "<="&EOMONTH(TODAY(),0))

Last month:
=SUMIFS(Revenue, Date, ">="&DATE(YEAR(TODAY()),MONTH(TODAY())-1,1), Date, "<="&EOMONTH(DATE(YEAR(TODAY()),MONTH(TODAY())-1,1),0))

Same month last year:
=SUMIFS(Revenue, Date, ">="&DATE(YEAR(TODAY())-1,MONTH(TODAY()),1), Date, "<="&EOMONTH(DATE(YEAR(TODAY())-1,MONTH(TODAY()),1),0))

All three update on the first of each month. You never change a date reference again.

Month over month change:
=IF(C2=0,"N/A",(B2-C2)/C2)
Format as percentage with one decimal. The IF handles the case where last month was zero.

Drop your time period comparison problem. Tell me what periods you are comparing and what your date column looks like and I will write the exact SUMIFS.

B) Intermediate — I can build most things but I hit real walls on complex problems
C) Advanced — I am the person people come to and I occasionally still get humbled
D) Dangerous — I have built things I cannot fully explain that are running in production right now

posts.content:
If you teach Excel or train analysts and you are not following @Jeff Lenning you are missing one of the most principled voices in this space.

Microsoft MVP. Founder of Excel University. Over 20 years teaching Excel to CPAs and accounting professionals.

His line from our Q&A in Excel Flow: Power Query is the single most underused tool in Excel among the professionals who would benefit from it the most.

He said it first. Four other top creators I have spoken to since have said the exact same thing independently.

Go follow him. Especially if you work in accounting, audit, or FP&A.

Genuine question for this group.

What is the one Excel feature you were intimidated by for way too long and then finally learned and could not believe you had been avoiding?

A) Power Query — I thought it was for data engineers and I was completely wrong about that
B) Dynamic arrays — I thought FILTER was complicated until I spent 20 minutes actually using it
C) Pivot tables — I avoided them for over a year before someone showed me they were not scary
D) Something else entirely and I am going to tell you exactly what it was in the comments

Drop the specific thing and how long you avoided it before finally learning it 👇

Two analysts. Same raw data. Two hours to deliver a report.

Analyst A builds a beautiful dashboard with slicers, dynamic charts, and conditional formatting. It works perfectly this week. Nobody can maintain it next week without them in the room.

Analyst B builds a clean, documented, slightly ugly but completely functional model with labeled inputs, clear calculation steps, and a README tab. It works perfectly this week and next month and six months after they leave the company.

Which analyst actually did the better job?

A) Analyst A — a beautiful dashboard gets used. An ugly model collects dust in a shared folder.
B) Analyst B — maintainability is the real skill. Anyone can make something look polished.
C) Neither — the right answer combines both and neither analyst made the right call
D) Depends entirely on whether they need to hand it off — if they keep it forever A wins

Drop your take and the real example behind it 👇

What is the Excel skill gap you would be most embarrassed for your manager to discover?

A) I do not actually understand how pivot tables calculate — I have been faking it with SUMIFS
B) My "automation" is just a very organized manual process
C) I have been using a formula for years and I cannot fully explain how it works
D) My Excel is excellent but my Power Query knowledge is genuinely zero

Vote. This is the most judgment-free community on Substack. We have all been here.

Your pivot table is grouping dates by month but you need them by week and nothing you have tried is working.

By default Excel automatically groups dates by year, quarter, and month. To change it:

Right click any date in the pivot table → Group → uncheck everything → check Days only → set Number of days to 7 → OK.

This groups by 7-day periods starting from your earliest date. Not perfect calendar weeks but close enough for most purposes.

For actual Monday-to-Sunday calendar weeks:
Add a helper column to your source data:
=A2-WEEKDAY(A2,2)+1

This returns the Monday of whatever week each date falls in. Use this helper column as your date field in the pivot instead of the raw date. Every group is now a proper calendar week.

Drop your pivot table date grouping problem. Tell me what period you need and what your date data looks like and I will give you the exact fix.

→ PivotTable advanced features in Excel Flow Edition #30
[link]

Real scenario:

"I have a list of invoices. Some have been paid, some are overdue, some are due soon. I need a status column that says Paid, Overdue, Due This Week, or Upcoming automatically."

One formula using IFS written cleanly:

=IFS(
C2<>"", "Paid",
B2<TODAY(), "Overdue",
B2<=TODAY()+7, "Due This Week",
TRUE, "Upcoming"
)

Where B2 is the due date and C2 is the paid date (blank if not yet paid).

How the logic works: it checks for a paid date first — if it exists the status is Paid regardless of the due date. Then it checks if the due date has passed. Then if it is within seven days. Everything else is Upcoming. TRUE at the end is the catch-all.

Add conditional formatting:
Paid → light green
Overdue → red
Due This Week → amber
Upcoming → no fill

The whole setup takes fifteen minutes and you never manually update a status again.

Drop your status column scenario. If your logic has more conditions I will build the exact IFS for your situation.

Drop what yours would actually say 👇

What is the Excel accomplishment from this week that nobody around you fully appreciated because they did not understand what it actually took?

A) I automated something that was eating two hours a week and nobody noticed because it just worked
B) I found an error in someone else's model that would have caused a serious problem
C) I built something clean and well-structured that everyone uses and nobody thinks about
D) I finally learned something I had been avoiding for months and used it the same day

Vote and drop the specific thing below. This community appreciates it even if nobody at work did.

You need to compare two lists and find what is in one but not the other. You have been doing this manually.

Flag mismatches with a formula:
=IF(COUNTIF($B$2:$B$1000, A2)=0, "Not in List B", "Found")
Filter by "Not in List B" to see everything missing.

Dynamic array version — full missing list automatically:
=FILTER(A2:A1000, COUNTIF(B2:B1000, A2:A1000)=0)
Spills every value from List A that does not appear in List B. Updates when either list changes.

Find values in BOTH lists:
=FILTER(A2:A1000, COUNTIF(B2:B1000, A2:A1000)>0)

Find values in EITHER list but not both:
=VSTACK(
FILTER(A2:A1000, COUNTIF(B2:B1000, A2:A1000)=0),
FILTER(B2:B1000, COUNTIF(A2:A1000, B2:B1000)=0)
)

Drop your list comparison problem. Tell me what the two lists contain and what the output needs to look like.

→ Dynamic array functions in Excel Flow Edition #27
https://substack.com/@rileystream/p-197635354

Real scenario:

"I have a large financial model with multiple scenarios. Right now I have a different tab for each scenario and I am maintaining three copies of the same model. When something changes I have to update all three."

Three proper approaches in order of complexity:

Option 1 — Scenario Manager (built in, no formulas)
Data → What-If Analysis → Scenario Manager → Add. Define which cells change and what values they take per scenario. Switch between scenarios with one click. Best for simple models with a small number of changing inputs.

Option 2 — Input table with a dropdown selector
Put all scenario inputs in one structured table. One row per scenario. Add a dropdown in a cell called ScenarioSelector. Every input cell in your model references the table using XLOOKUP keyed to the selector. Change the dropdown and the entire model updates instantly. One model. Zero duplicate tabs. This is what most serious financial modelers converge on.

Option 3 — Data Tables for sensitivity analysis
Data → What-If Analysis → Data Table. Creates a matrix showing how outputs change across a range of inputs automatically. Best for continuous ranges rather than discrete named scenarios.

Option 2 is the permanent fix. One model is always better than three.

Drop your scenario setup. Tell me how many inputs change between scenarios and I will tell you which approach fits your exact situation.

Your personal budget spreadsheet is overcomplicated and you stopped updating it three weeks ago.

Here is the version that actually gets used:

Five columns you actually need:
Date — Category — Description — Amount — Running Total

That is it. Everything else is noise until you have been consistent for three months.

Running total:
=SUM($D$2:D2) — drag down and it updates automatically with every new row.

Monthly summary:
=SUMIFS(D:D, A:A, ">="&DATE(2026,6,1), A:A, "<="&EOMONTH(DATE(2026,6,1),0))

Category breakdown:
A pivot table with Category as rows and Amount as values. Refresh once a week. Takes thirty seconds.

The one rule that makes the whole system work:
Enter every transaction the day it happens. Two minutes a day beats a two-hour Sunday catch-up every time. The spreadsheet only works if the data is current.

Drop the personal finance feature you built and actually use versus the one you built and completely abandoned.

Real scenario:

"I have 12 months of bank statements as PDFs. I do not want to type every transaction in manually."

Four options in order of speed:

Option 1 — CSV export from your bank
Most banks have a transaction history download option. CSV format opens directly in Excel. Check this before anything else. This is the right answer for 90% of people.

Option 2 — Power Query PDF connector
Data → Get Data → From File → From PDF. Power Query detects tables in the PDF and imports them. Works on most standard bank statement formats.

Option 3 — Export from Adobe Acrobat
Open the PDF → Export → Spreadsheet → Excel. Works when the PDF contains real table data rather than scanned images.

Option 4 — Scanned image PDF
Copy all text (Ctrl+A, Ctrl+C), paste into Excel, use Flash Fill (Ctrl+E) to extract dates, amounts, and descriptions from the raw text. Takes about fifteen minutes per statement but it works.

Tell me what bank and what format your statements are in and I will give you the exact path.

You are writing formulas that work perfectly today but break the moment someone inserts a row, adds a column, or renames a sheet.

Here is how to build formulas that are structurally bulletproof:

Use Tables instead of ranges.
=SUM(SalesData[Revenue]) never breaks when rows are added.
=SUM(B2:B100) breaks the moment row 101 exists.

Use named ranges for constants.
Name your tax rate cell TaxRate. Your formula reads =Revenue*TaxRate instead of =B2*$F$3. If the cell ever moves the named range follows it automatically.

Avoid INDIRECT unless you have no other option.
INDIRECT is volatile — it recalculates on every single keystroke and breaks if a sheet it references gets renamed. Prefer Table references.

Avoid OFFSET as a last resort.
Also volatile. FILTER and dynamic arrays replace 90% of OFFSET use cases in modern Excel.

The formula that breaks least often is the one that references a Table column by name rather than a cell address by location.

Drop the formula you have that you know is fragile. Tell me why it breaks and I will rewrite it to be structurally sound.

→ Model structure and formula discipline in Excel Flow Edition #26
https://rileystream.substack.com/p/carl-seidman-why-excel-skills-dont

Real scenario:

"I run a small business and I need to know which products are in the top 20% of revenue and which are in the bottom 20%. I have 200 products."

Two formulas, one for each threshold:

Top 20% threshold:
=PERCENTILE($B$2:$B$201, 0.8)
Returns the revenue value at the 80th percentile. Everything above this is in your top 20%.

Flag each product automatically:
=IFS(
B2>=PERCENTILE($B$2:$B$201,0.8), "Top 20%",
B2<=PERCENTILE($B$2:$B$201,0.2), "Bottom 20%",
TRUE, "Middle 60%"
)

Tags every product in one formula. No helper columns. No manual thresholds.

Add conditional formatting:
Top 20% → green fill
Bottom 20% → red fill
Middle 60% → no fill

The labels update automatically if your revenue numbers change. Add a new product to the table and the percentile thresholds recalculate and every label updates with them.

Drop your product or customer segmentation scenario. If you need more than three tiers or weighted percentiles I will build the full formula in the comments.
